does: This paper provides the first quantitative scaling analysis of reward models (ranging from 1.3B to 7B parameters) trained on human feedback with diverse preferences, revealing that model capacity and data size interact to determine how well reward models handle preference diversity. The authors introduce Expected Calibration Error (ECE) as a metric for evaluating reward model quality and demonstrate its positive correlation with downstream LLM alignment performance. They propose Multi-Objective Reward learning (MORE) as a method to improve reward model calibration when facing diversified human preferences, showing empirical gains in alignment effectiveness across different model sizes.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on LLM alignment through reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), building on prior work examining reward model training and the challenges of aggregating diverse human judgments. It responds to earlier observations that human preferences often conflict or vary across annotators, a problem largely treated as noise in standard RLHF pipelines. The paper connects to scaling law research in deep learning by investigating how model capacity affects robustness to preference diversity, and draws on calibration literature from classical machine learning to propose ECE as a diagnostic tool for reward model quality.
enables: This work enables more robust LLM alignment systems that explicitly account for the reality of pluralistic human values rather than treating preference diversity as a problem to average away. By establishing the connection between reward model calibration and alignment performance, it opens new research directions in designing reward models that gracefully handle heterogeneous preferences. The MORE method and ECE metric provide tools for future work to develop alignment approaches that preserve or leverage diverse human feedback rather than homogenizing it, potentially supporting LLMs that can serve diverse user populations or explicitly navigate value pluralism.
